original_abstract: A central challenge in the emerging field of molecular spintronics lies in identifying new spin qubit platforms capable of meeting the rigorous demands of quantum information science. Recent studies suggest that photoexcited chromophore-radical systems are particularly promising candidates for expanding this chemical repertoire. In this work, we employ transient electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy to investigate a series of three molecular architectures featuring a perylene diimide (PDI) chromophore covalently linked to a stable 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidin-1-oxyl (TEMPO) radical. By systematically exploring the effects of isotope labeling within the TEMPO moiety, we characterize the EPR signatures and electron spin coherence of the photo-generated molecular quartet states. Our results demonstrate that the spin states of these systems can be coherently manipulated even at room temperature, achieving a spin coherence time ($T_2$) of 0.7 μs. This observation of robust electron spin coherence under ambient conditions represents a significant step toward the integration of molecular qubits into functional, real-world quantum devices.
humanized_abstract: Molecular spintronics aims to utilize molecular species as information storage media, which is generally considered to be in the remote future because the current spin qubits are not compatible with the requirements of quantum information science. Alternative spin qubits are therefore needed urgently. Photoexcited chromophore–radical hybrid systems may open a new avenue in this context. Here, we have used transient EPR to investigate three novel molecular hybrids in which a perylene diimide (PDI) chromophore is attached to a stable 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidin-1-oxyl (TEMPO) radical. The influences of different labeling of the TEMPO moiety were systematically investigated, allowing the EPR signals of photoexcited molecular quartet states to be identified. It was possible to show that the spin states can be controlled at room temperature with a spin coherence time (T 2) of 0.7 μs, which is a major milestone on the way to the implementation of molecular qubits in actual quantum devices.
